compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
Plan-review advisory dispositions (v3 PASS, 0 blocking, 20 advisory). The advisories are specification-precision refinements; the material ones are ACCEPTED and will be reflected in the implementation (and in the test oracle) before the change receives its Gerrit votes:
- ACCEPT (evidence pipeline, ~0.67/0.33): the consolidation job + fragment names (evidence-shapin/evidence-build/evidence-mcp) are specified concretely â€” a final 'consolidate-evidence' job that needs: the fragment-producing jobs, downloads all evidence-* artifacts, and concatenates them into release-evidence.txt; test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f) asserts the fragment names + their write commands + the consolidation job's download/concat wiring.
- ACCEPT (check_build_env_locked base allowlist, ~0.67/0.54): the allowlist is exactly {pip, setuptools, wheel} (lowercased), documented as a constant in the script with a comment; the script fails on any installed pkg absent from the lock OR version-mismatched, EXCEPT those three; unit test feeds a clean fixture (passes) and an extra-package fixture (fails).
- ACCEPT (actionlint install, ~0.54/0.26): actionlint is installed in a Makefile prerequisite via a pinned-version GitHub release download + sha256sum -c against a hard-coded digest (dogfooding the same digest-verify discipline), pinned to a specific actionlint version; the Makefile target is idempotent (skips if the pinned binary is already present).
- REJECT (0.40 generic 'AC/DoD quality'): too vague to action; the ACs are concrete + test-backed after the two BLOCK remediations.
Remaining lower-impact advisories are consistency/wording notes folded into the same concrete specifications above.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
[operator-attested] evidence â€” mcp-publisher pin correctness. Ran `make verify-mcp-pin` in-session (independently, not just the implementer): it downloads the exact pinned URL and asserts byte-for-byte digest equality. Output:
  pinned URL    = https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases/download/v1.8.0/mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz
  pinned SHA256 = 1370446bbe74d562608e8005a6ccce02d146a661fbd78674e11cc70b9618d6cf
  downloaded    = 1370446bbe74d562608e8005a6ccce02d146a661fbd78674e11cc70b9618d6cf (7337300 bytes)
  RESULT: OK â€” the pinned digest matches the live download.
The digest also matches upstream registry_1.8.0_checksums.txt (confirmed by the implementer). So the embedded SHA-256 is correct for the pinned URL (not just that the sha256sum -c mechanism works).

Held-out TDD validation: full oracle 16 passed / 3 skipped locally (the sha256sum -c tests skip off-GNU; run in CI on ubuntu). Real zizmor: 'No findings (1 suppressed = the annotated excessive-permissions on mcp_registry)'; teeth-verified â€” unpinning one action makes zizmor report error[unpinned-uses] (high) AND the structural test fail. Real actionlint: clean. make lint + typecheck clean.

compiled_state.description:
# Pin the release supply chain to immutable references

Make every executable the release depends on identifiable by an immutable
reference: full-SHA action pins, a hash-locked build environment, an
exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher, and non-persisted checkout
credentials. (Readiness Finding 4 â€” verified VALID against `release.yml`
lines 31/32/43/60/65/85/124/129 and `pyproject.toml:1-3`.)

## Why

Two release runs of the same rebar SHA can execute different build/upload/publish
code today. `release.yml` references first-party actions by moving major-version
tags (`actions/checkout@v7` at :31/:124, `actions/setup-python@v6` at :32,
`actions/upload-artifact@v7` at :43, `actions/download-artifact@v8` at :60/:85);
installs build tools with unrestricted upgrades (`pip install --upgrade build`
:37, `--upgrade twine` :41); publishes via the branch-like `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1`
(:65); declares the build backend as bare `hatchling` (`pyproject.toml:1-3`); and
in the `mcp_registry` job downloads its publisher from a mutable `releases/latest`
URL and executes it **without a digest/signature check** (:129) in a job that holds
`id-token: write` (used for GitHub OIDC login + registry publication). None of the
checkouts set `persist-credentials: false`, so the token persists in local git
config by default. The repo SHA-pins **some** third-party actions in
`gerrit-verify.yaml` (its header claims comprehensive pinning, but e.g.
`gerrit-verify.yaml:75` still has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6`), so the
generic action-security checks are not yet enforced on `release.yml`. Rather than
hand-roll regex checks, this story adopts the **proven, maintained tools** â€”
`zizmor` (GitHub-Actions security auditor: unpinned actions, credential persistence,
over-broad/dangerous permissions incl. OIDC) and `actionlint` â€” run under
`make lint` **scoped to `release.yml`**, plus a thin project-specific test for the
rebar-only invariants zizmor doesn't know about. (Fixing `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s own
unpinned `setup-python` and repo-wide zizmor adoption are a separate follow-up;
path-scoping the invocation to `release.yml` keeps this story bounded and avoids
breaking `make lint` on out-of-scope pre-existing findings.)

## What

Adopt the uv / pip pinning pattern (uv: full-SHA action pins, `persist-credentials:
false`, a pinned release tool with a verified SHA-256; pip: SHA-pinned actions +
a hash-locked build environment installed with `--require-hashes --no-deps`):

- Replace each action tag with a full commit SHA, keeping the human-readable
  version in a trailing comment.
- Set `persist-credentials: false` on every release checkout unless a later git
  operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential.
- **Enforce the generic action-security checks with `zizmor` + `actionlint` (proven
  tools, not hand-rolled).** Add `zizmor` (itself version+hash-pinned in the release
  lock) and `actionlint` to `make lint`, **invoked with `release.yml` as an explicit
  path argument** (not repo-wide), so they run under the Gerrit `Verified` gate.
  `zizmor` enforces full-SHA pinning of `uses:`, `persist-credentials: false`, and
  over-broad/dangerous permissions (including OIDC) on `release.yml`. **One annotated
  exception:** `mcp_registry`'s `id-token: write` is consumed by the opaque shell command
  `mcp-publisher login github-oidc`, which zizmor cannot introspect, so it false-positives
  as excessive â€” suppressed with a single scoped
  `# zizmor:ignore[excessive-permissions] -- â€¦` on that job's `permissions:` block (the
  only suppression allowed; the test asserts it verbatim and forbids any other). A thin
  `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` (also under `make test`) then asserts
  only the **rebar-specific** invariants `zizmor` cannot know: the `--no-isolation`
  build flag, the `mcp_verify`(no `id-token`)/`mcp_registry`(has it) split, the
  `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step, the evidence-fragment wiring, and the
  `docs/releasing.md` "Pinning" section substrings.
- Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file covering `build`, Hatchling,
  Twine, any artifact-validation tools, and their transitive deps, generated and
  maintained with an established tool â€” **`pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools)**
  (or `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`) â€” checked into the repo.
- **Hatchling enforcement â€” decision.** pip's default `python -m build` resolves the
  `[build-system]` requires in a *fresh isolated env*, so the lock only governs
  Hatchling's version if the build uses the pre-installed locked env. **This story
  uses `--no-isolation`**: pre-install the hash-locked env (including Hatchling)
  with `pip install --require-hashes -r .github/release-requirements.txt`, then `python -m build
  --no-isolation`. This keeps enforcement entirely in the workflow and avoids
  editing `pyproject.toml` `[build-system]`. **Close the ambient-package hole with a
  parsed comparison (not a raw diff).** Because `ubuntu-latest` ships
  `pip`/`setuptools`/`wheel`/`packaging` and the `--no-isolation` build runs in the
  ambient env, build in a **fresh venv** containing only the hash-locked packages,
  then run a small guard script (`scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`) that: parses the
  installed set from `pip freeze` (lowercased `name`â†’`version`), parses the expected
  `name==version` entries from `.github/release-requirements.txt` (skipping `--hash`
  and comment lines â€” the two files are NOT raw-diff-comparable), and **fails if any
  installed package is absent from the lock or version-mismatched**, modulo a small
  documented base allowlist (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`). This is a deterministic
  parsed check, not a text diff of the two file formats. The `test_release_workflow_pins.py` test (run by `make test`) fails if
  `--no-isolation` is absent (so no free-resolution path can silently return).
- **MCP publisher â€” download â†’ verify â†’ execute (never stream into tar).** Replace
  the `releases/latest` URL with an exact version, and split the current
  `curl â€¦ | tar xz` one-liner into three ordered steps so an unverified archive is
  never extracted or executed:
  1. `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`
  2. `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c --strict`
  3. `tar xzf mcp-publisher.tar.gz mcp-publisher`
  Prefer an upstream `checksums.txt` if `modelcontextprotocol/registry` ships one;
  otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time with a rationale comment.
  **Arch note:** the current download URL is built from `uname -s`/`uname -m`, so the
  `<url, sha256>` pair is arch-specific. `release.yml` runs on `ubuntu-latest`
  (linux/amd64), so pin the `linux_amd64` URL + its SHA-256 as a single deliberate
  pair (documented, with a revisit note if the runner arch changes); store one pair
  per supported OS/arch tuple if the matrix ever expands.
  **Security contract â€” structural job split (not step ordering).** This follows
  GitHub's own *Security hardening for GitHub Actions* guidance (least-privilege
  permissions; isolate privileged operations into their own job) and the PyPA
  Trusted-Publishing model of minimizing what runs under the OIDC token. In GitHub
  Actions `id-token: write` is a *job-level* permission: the OIDC token endpoint
  (`$ACTIONS_ID_TOKEN_REQUEST_URL`) is reachable from the job's **first** step, so
  step ordering within one job does NOT prevent a compromised archive from minting a
  token during the download step. Enforce co-residency structurally by **splitting
  the job in two**: `mcp_verify` (no `id-token: write`; runs curl download +
  `sha256sum -c` and uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified`
  output) and `mcp_registry` (`needs: mcp_verify`; holds `id-token: write`; consumes
  the already-verified artifact and runs `mcp-publisher login`/`publish`). Fail on an
  unexpected filename/size/digest/version. So the OIDC-privileged job never downloads
  or holds an unverified binary. **Restore the executable bit after the handoff:**
  `actions/upload-artifact` (v3+) does not preserve POSIX permissions, so
  `mcp_registry` must `chmod +x mcp-publisher` after `download-artifact` or the
  `./mcp-publisher login` call fails "Permission denied" at runtime.
- Print and retain release evidence. GitHub Actions artifacts are **job-scoped** and
  cannot be appended to across jobs, so each job uploads its **own distinctly-named**
  fragment (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`; Finding 3 later adds
  `evidence-artifacts` for the wheel/sdist sums), and a final aggregation step downloads all fragments and concatenates them into one
  `release-evidence.txt` â€” recording every action SHA, the Python build-tool versions,
  the MCP publisher version + verified digest, and the wheel/sdist digests.
- Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes, not implicit resolution
  during a production release.

## Scope

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` â€” SHA-pin all `uses:`; `persist-credentials:
  false`; `--no-isolation` hash-locked build install; split the MCP publisher
  into an unprivileged `mcp_verify` job + an OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job that
  consumes the verified artifact (coordinate with Findings 1 and 3, which also edit
  this file).
- `.github/release-requirements.txt` (new, the hash-locked constraints file) compiled
  from `.github/release-requirements.in` via
  `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`.
- `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` (new) â€” the parsed installed-vs-lock guard
  (lowercased `name==version` comparison, base allowlist), with a unit test over a
  fixture lock+freeze pair.
- `scripts/verify_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (new) + a `make verify-mcp-pin` target â€” parses the
  pinned mcp-publisher URL + embedded SHA-256 out of `release.yml`, downloads the URL,
  computes its SHA-256, and asserts equality (non-zero on mismatch). The implementer runs it
  once in-session to confirm the embedded hash is self-consistent with the pinned URL and
  records the passing output on the ticket (the `[operator-attested]` AC above); its
  *existence + wiring* is asserted by `test_release_workflow_pins.py`.
- `Makefile` + `pyproject.toml [dev]` â€” add `zizmor` and `actionlint` to `make lint`,
  **invoked scoped to `release.yml` specifically** (`zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml`
  and `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml`), NOT repo-wide â€” otherwise they would
  flag pre-existing out-of-scope issues in other workflows (e.g. `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`'s
  unpinned `setup-python@v6`) and break the very Gerrit `Verified` gate that runs
  `make lint`. (Repo-wide `zizmor`/`actionlint` adoption is a separate follow-up
  ticket.) **Concrete install paths** (the gate installs via `pip install -e '.[dev]'`,
  so an unfound tool would fail `command not found`): `zizmor` is pip-installable â€” pin
  it in `pyproject.toml [dev]` (and hash-pin it in the release lock for the release-lint
  usage); `actionlint` is a Go binary â€” install it via a **pinned-version GitHub release
  download + SHA-256 verification** in a `Makefile` prerequisite (dogfooding the same
  digest-verify discipline this story establishes for `mcp-publisher`). Scoped to
  `release.yml`, `zizmor` enforces the **generic** checks:
  full-SHA pinning of every `uses:`, `persist-credentials: false`, and
  over-broad/dangerous permissions (incl. OIDC). A standalone PR-triggered GitHub
  workflow would NOT run pre-merge in this repo's Gerrit flow, which is why the lint
  runs under `make lint`, not a `pull_request` trigger.
- `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` (new) â€” a thin test under `make test`
  for only the **rebar-specific** invariants `zizmor` can't know, parsing
  `release.yml` + `docs/releasing.md`: (c) the build invocation includes
  `--no-isolation`; (d) the `docs/releasing.md` "Pinning" section contains the three
  required substrings (see the docs AC); (e) the job-permission split â€”
  `mcp_verify` declares no `id-token: write`, `mcp_registry` does (fail if
  `mcp_verify` gains it); (f) the release-evidence wiring â€” the three fragment names
  this story delivers (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp`) appear in
  `release.yml` and a consolidation job downloads + concatenates them
  (`evidence-artifacts` is asserted by Finding 3's story after it lands); (g) a
  `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step is present in the `mcp_registry` job; (h) the
  ambient-package guard step (`scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` â€” a parsed
  nameâ†’version comparison, NOT a raw text diff) is present.
- `tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (new) â€” run by `make test`; writes a test
  copy of the pinned archive, corrupts one byte, runs the `mcp_verify` `sha256sum -c`
  logic, and asserts a non-zero exit.
- `pyproject.toml` â€” **no change** to `[build-system]` requires: with the chosen
  `--no-isolation` strategy the Hatchling version is governed by the
  pre-installed hash-locked env, not by `pyproject.toml` (this is deliberate; if the
  isolated-build path were kept instead, a `[build-system]` pin would be required â€”
  but it is not kept).
- `docs/releasing.md` â€” document the pinning policy and the pin-update process.
  **Section ownership:** this story owns the "Pinning" section only (Finding 1 owns
  "Release-source authorization", Finding 3 owns "Build-once"), avoiding overlap
  across the three concurrent release stories.
- Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned; full reproducible
  builds (covered adjacent to Finding 3).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Every `uses:` in `release.yml` references a full 40-char commit SHA with a
  trailing version comment â€” enforced by `zizmor` (via `make lint`), which fails on
  any unpinned `uses:`.
- [ ] Every release checkout sets `persist-credentials: false` â€” enforced by `zizmor`
  (via `make lint`), which flags credential persistence.
- [ ] `zizmor` + `actionlint` run under `make lint` **scoped to `release.yml`** (explicit
  path argument, not repo-wide â€” so pre-existing out-of-scope findings like
  `gerrit-verify.yaml:75` don't break the gate), with concrete install paths: `zizmor`
  pinned in `pyproject.toml [dev]` (+ hash-pinned in the release lock) and `actionlint`
  installed via a pinned-version release download with SHA-256 verification in a
  `Makefile` prerequisite; `make lint` finds both in CI (no `command not found`).
  `zizmor` reports **no findings on `release.yml` except a single annotated suppression**
  for `mcp_registry`'s `id-token: write` (SHA-pins, persist-credentials, and all other
  over-broad/OIDC permissions clean). The one suppression is unavoidable and legitimate:
  `mcp_registry` genuinely needs `id-token: write` for `mcp-publisher login github-oidc`,
  but because that is an **opaque shell command** (not a `uses:` step zizmor can
  introspect), zizmor's `excessive-permissions` audit cannot see the token being consumed
  and reports a false positive. It is suppressed with an inline
  `# zizmor:ignore[excessive-permissions] -- id-token: write is consumed by
  mcp-publisher login github-oidc (runtime OIDC token request; zizmor cannot introspect
  shell commands)` on the `mcp_registry` `permissions:` block. `test_release_workflow_pins.py`
  asserts this exact suppression comment is present (so it cannot be silently broadened to
  suppress other findings), and that no OTHER `zizmor:ignore` appears in `release.yml`.
- [ ] Release build tools (`build`, Hatchling, Twine, validation tools) install from
  `.github/release-requirements.txt` (generated by `pip-compile --generate-hashes
  --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`)
  with `--require-hashes` â€” the install itself is the verification: `pip install
  --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` re-hashes every artifact against the lock and
  **fails the build** if any package was replaced or its hash doesn't match (a
  `--dry-run` would not, since pip skips digest-checking for already-satisfied
  packages). The install step emits `pip list --format=freeze > tool-versions.txt`
  into the release evidence artifact.
- [ ] After the hash-locked install, `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` performs a
  **parsed** comparison â€” installed packages (from `pip freeze`, lowercased
  nameâ†’version) vs the lock's `name==version` entries (skipping `--hash`/comment
  lines) â€” and fails on any installed package absent from the lock or version-
  mismatched, modulo the documented `pip`/`setuptools`/`wheel` base allowlist (closing
  the ambient-package hole under `--no-isolation --no-deps`; a raw text diff of the two
  formats would be meaningless). `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (h) asserts this
  guard step is wired into `release.yml`, and a unit test feeds the script a
  lock+freeze fixture pair to confirm it passes on a clean env and fails on an extra
  package.
- [ ] Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via `--no-isolation` against
  the pre-installed hash-locked env (not via a `pyproject.toml` pin), with
  `test_release_workflow_pins.py` failing if `--no-isolation` is removed from the build
  invocation (no free-resolution path remains).
- [ ] The MCP publisher is pinned to an exact version and its archive digest is
  verified in a separate `mcp_verify` job that has **no** `id-token: write`; the
  OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job `needs: mcp_verify` and consumes only the
  verified artifact â€” so the OIDC scope is never co-resident with an unverified
  binary; `mcp_registry` runs `chmod +x mcp-publisher` after `download-artifact`
  (upload-artifact drops the exec bit). `test_release_workflow_pins.py` additionally
  asserts the permission structure (`mcp_verify` has no `id-token: write`,
  `mcp_registry` does) and that the `chmod +x mcp-publisher` step is present. Verified by `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py`, which
  corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits
  non-zero. If upstream ships no checksums, the pinned SHA-256 is hard-coded in
  the workflow with a rationale comment.
- [ ] [operator-attested] The embedded SHA-256 is **correct for the pinned URL** (not just
  that the verify *mechanism* works): a new `scripts/verify_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (invokable
  as `make verify-mcp-pin`) parses the exact pinned download URL and the embedded SHA-256 out
  of `release.yml`, downloads that URL, computes its SHA-256, and asserts byte-for-byte
  equality with the embedded value â€” exiting non-zero on any mismatch. `test_release_workflow_pins.py`
  asserts the script exists and that `make verify-mcp-pin` is wired (codebase-verifiable); the
  actual network download + hash match is `[operator-attested]` â€” the implementer runs
  `make verify-mcp-pin` once in-session and records its passing output (the computed digest ==
  the embedded digest) as a comment on this ticket, so a typoed / wrong-arch / stale hash is
  caught before the change lands rather than at release runtime.
- [ ] The release-evidence pipeline is **wired** so each job writes its data to a
  distinctly-named fragment (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`) and a
  consolidation job concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt` (job-scoped
  artifacts cannot be appended across jobs). `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (f)
  asserts the *wiring* statically â€” the fragment names **and** the write commands that
  populate them (e.g. the mcp step redirecting version+digest into `evidence-mcp`), so
  an empty-file implementation fails the check. The *populated content* of a real run
  is `[operator-attested]`: the releaser attaches the run's `release-evidence.txt` to
  the release ticket. (Wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums are added as the `evidence-artifacts`
  fragment by Finding 3's build-once step, which lands **after** this story, so they
  are out of scope here.)
- [ ] `docs/releasing.md` contains a "Pinning" section covering, at minimum: (a) the
  rationale for full-SHA action pins, (b) the command to regenerate the lock file
  (`pip-compile --generate-hashes â€¦`), and (c) how to update the MCP publisher pin
  (version + SHA-256) â€” verified by `test_release_workflow_pins.py` asserting all three
  exact substrings are present in `docs/releasing.md`: `pip-compile --generate-hashes`,
  `MCP publisher`, and `full-SHA` (the pinning rationale keyword). Each is a literal
  `grep -q` so the check is deterministic.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass, and the change lands via the
  Gerrit two-vote gate.

file_impact.reason: SHA-pin actions, persist-credentials, hash-locked build, digest-verified publisher

compiled_state.title: Pin the release supply chain to immutable references
